speaker: NORLA CALLISON
words: We've had lots of winters here where we never got much snowuntil Christmas. Well, one thing we can think about, since it started earlylike this maybe we'll have an early spring. But that got so it don't work.

speaker: WALT BENSCOTER
words: That don't hold true always.

speaker: NORLA
words: It used to. And there's another thing that we used to have that wedon't have any more. We had one a year or two ago and that's January thaw.

speaker: WALT
words: January thaw.

speaker: NORLA
words: When I was a kid we would get snow knee deep. And people would say,"Oh well, we'd get the January thaw and take it off" and it was just as sure asdeath and taxes.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah.

speaker: NORLA
words: We got that January thaw, it never missed. But we don't get themJanuary thaws. We had one a year two ago, a small one, but that's the firstJanuary thaw we've had in years. Our winters are changing. I don't knowwhether cutting off the timber changed things or not. I know back in theCedar Creek, the Crescent country, since they cut the timber off there it'snot near as frosty in there. It let the air currents in there, it's a lotwarmer. But our seasons are changing.

speaker: WALT
words: Listen Bub, don't you think that the whole cockeyed thing is changing?6H3RLA: I think so.

speaker: WALT
words: You can remember years ago when we used to farm with horses. We hadto get in the field early in order to get our work done. And we did. Wewas able to get out there well...

speaker: NORLA
words: Well if it's like it's been now, ue^had to have to farm with horses. Ittook a week or so get your horses toughened in or you'd kill 'em.

speaker: WALT
words: That's right.

speaker: NORLA
words: Why you wouldn't get to first base. (No.) Plowing with horses now.Just about a week now that its fit to work.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah.

speaker: NORLA
words: It's too wet, and then there's about a week it's fit to work, then it'stoo dry. When we worked with horses it wasn't that way.

speaker: WALT
words: No it was better. Plow for days.

speaker: NORLA
words: I remember the spring of 1934, why I remember it very distinctly. Thatwas the spring after we bought our first cat, we bought it the fall of '33.It was a mild winter and I plowed this piece of ground right across overhere in February. It was warm of an afternoon going along that north slope,and I could look down this way and the ground was covered with cobwebs andthere was little gnats. The air was full of 'em and that was in February...

speaker: WALT
words: Just like in the fall of the year.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's right, just like fall of the year. Them cobwebs and them gnats,the air was full of 'em.ROB MOORE: You'd be plowing that soon, in February?

speaker: NORLA
words: I did that year. And that's the last time I've ever plowed in February,in 1934, the year after I bought the cat. That's why I know it what I boughtit in the fall of '33.

speaker: WALT
words: That same spring Bub, I think it was, I'm quite sure it was the same spring,We went to work in February and we never quit until everything is done. Wenever had a, you know, didn't have any more bad weather or have any winterat all.NORLA: When I was going to school over here...I finished going to school overhere in 1918 and you know there was more than one year when I was going toschool over there that the Claus Eichner would seed in February. (Yeah.)'Course it was early down on that canyon point down there, but I know thathappened more than once. The kids would come to school, "Well, dad's seeding."That was in February. 'Course to get to seeding now in February...ROB: January and February now is usually when...

speaker: NORLA
words: ...when we have weather.

speaker: WALT
words: One of the hardest months. I think the whole thing is changing.

speaker: NORLA
words: I think our weather pattern has changed. (Yeah.) I don't think there'sany doubt about it.ROB: Does that mean a difference in the crops that people put in?

speaker: WALT
words: Well yes and no. We used to be able to grow spring wheat here. We'dgrow real good spring wheat. I don't think you could get your money backnow if you tried it. It turns so quick and there's no summer moisture.

speaker: NORLA
words: One time, and I can't think of the year but it was after I bought the cat.That was fall of '33. It was after that several years. But I had theWhetstine place in fall wheat and...by golly it was after Mabel and I wasmarried. We got married in the fall of '34. By golly I had that seededin fall wheat and it winter-killed. Just a piece of standing, it looked likea.•.well it was a mess. It was just no wheat there.

speaker: WALT
words: Yah, yah. It winter-killed early in the fall I'll betcha.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yes, it did.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. I...

speaker: NORLA
words: It was no good at all. And by golly 'course now I was going to turn itover and it turned then and we had a wet spring and I couldn't dig it up.That's all I had to put in to amount to anything. And you know when I gotthat in? It rained and rained and I just couldn't work, couldn't work, couldn'twork. And finally it quit raining the last of May, and I went over thereand dug that mess up and I remember I finished up, I seeded my corners outand it started to rain. Shower, it was wet, too wet to really be seeding,but I had to do it. And that was 5th of June.

speaker: WALT
words: 5th of June.

speaker: NORLA
words: 5th of June. I never forgot that. It was the 5th day of June that Iseeded that to Federation wheat. Federation used to be a good spring wheat.(Oh yeah.) I seeded that to Federation and so help me I got forty bushelsof spring wheat.

speaker: WALT
words: No fertilizer.

speaker: NORLA
words: Absolutely no fertilizer. Forty bushels, it was nice. I finished upin a shower seeding it. But I can't tell you the year but it was beforeMabel and I got married.

speaker: WALT
words: The last spring wheat that I ever grew, and that was pretty good croptoo, it was '41. That was the wet fall wasn't it? (Yeah.) '41.

speaker: NORLA
words: That was the year Warney and I went up and helped cut neighbor Cain'swheat up here by the balanced rock. '41. By golly we finished up and thatwheat was as soft as mush and it didn't taste like wheat.

speaker: WALT
words: It was chalky.

speaker: NORLA
words: It just tasted chalky and by God poor old Bob was beside himself to getit cut. And Warney and I went up there and worked one day, the two of us.That was big business in them days, two combines in one field. He had theold...I forget what combine he had. I had the old Case. But anyway we finishedup along when it was getting pretty in the evening like. And you know itwas so wet them combine tracks was that deep, sinking in. Talk about pullinghard!

speaker: WALT
words: Steel wheels.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's right steel wheels. Talk about pulling hard, you get a bulktank pretty full of wheat and you could see every one of them tracks rightout on the field. And them steel wheels was cutting in just about that deep.ROB: About three or four inches.

speaker: NORLA
words: When we finished up it was a'showering and that was the last...it rainedand there was never another kernel cut around here. But of course the restof us was done. Bob didn't have a combine. He was at the mercy of theweather. (Yeah.) But it was in October I know that.

speaker: WALT
words: We cut barley, around 28 acres on the Meyer's place and we could onlyget the bulk tank about half full. The front wheel would slide. And thefront wheel on the combine would slide.

speaker: NORLA
words: You take that wet wheat swelled up like that and that chalky stuff, bygolly it was so wet it wouldn't hardily run. Remember in the tank? (Yeah.)It just wouldn't run. Like dry wheat will just swish and go on like that.ROB: Wheat like that wouldn't be worth much to sell would it?

speaker: NORLA
words: There isn't hardly no food value in it. After wheat gets wet so manytimes it takes all the food value out of it.

speaker: WALT
words: Well hell, a lot of it sprouted, sprouted right in the head. No, Ifed a whole bunch of hogs that winter...

speaker: NORLA
words: They just stand around and squeal at it.

speaker: WALT
words: They just didn't do nothing.

speaker: NORLA
words: Everytime you'd go out there the hogs would be at the feeder there orat the fence looking up at you squealing. There's no food value to it.

speaker: WALT
words: You can feed them all you want to. I feed mine with a self-feeder thatnever did run out, but no growth.

speaker: NORLA
words: No they won't grow.

speaker: WALT
words: I had three old sows farrow the next spring. And they farrowed 31 pigs,the three of them, and I saved just one. The rest of them was either borndead or died just shortly after they were born.NORLA: The mother wasn't getting the right minerals.

speaker: WALT
words: Nope she didn't. If I'd a known, if I'd a had any idea, but I'd neverfed any of that kind of feed before.

speaker: NORLA
words: That was the same year that George Davidson had this piece over hereat Bob's, you know, across the draw. (Yeah.)

speaker: NORLA
words: It corners over there where Harley's and you and I all corner up. Hehad that Virginia Club wheat and that was one of the best fields of VirginiaClub I ever saw before it all fell down. It hit George at the chin. Boy,that was good wheat. It got wet and rained and they thrashed and harvestedand he had a mess of pigs there, and you know them pigs never done nothing.And before spring they all got sick, you remember? (Yeah.) And they hadpigs die like flies.

speaker: WALT
words: They just shipped fem in there from Montana.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah, they brought in some disease. If he didn't have one mess. Andthen feeding that crappy wheat with no food value in it and he got diseasefrom them Montanas mixed in with his pigs and he just lost hogs like...theydied like flies.ROB: I heard from a woman down in Genesee that the way her grandfather savedhimself in the wet harvest of 1893 was that he turned his hogs loose inthe stacks. In the wheat stacks. And that's the only way the he managed tomake anything out of it.

speaker: NORLA
words: I've heard of people just turning them out in the field. You know JohnWoody used to turn his hogs out every fall, 'course that was a good fall.They'd run and pick up every head and they'd fatten up man them pigs...

speaker: WALT
words: No, I'll differ with you. The Meyer place was hog-tied. I thought Ihad it made.

speaker: NORLA
words: Well, John used to run his on his and his wasn't hog-tied. Run over thecanyon-side and everywhere.WALT: Well by damn I tried her and I don't think they gained five pounds allfall.

speaker: NORLA
words: Remember Sam Bigham used to always turn his pigs out. (Yeah.) Everyfall Sam...

speaker: WALT
words: Bub, they just run. You know what I mean they just...they run so muchthat they don't...

speaker: NORLA
words: They run their legs off looking for 'em.

speaker: WALT
words: Well, I'll tell you it was little different when they used to shockbecause the heads would all be around the shock.

speaker: NORLA
words: Around the shock.

speaker: WALT
words: But when you combining it was a different story.

speaker: NORLA
words: Well, I'm speaking about thrashing with a stationary. I know John Woodyand Sam Bigham, they pretty much had their places all fenced except along thecanyon. There was nothing down there and they wouldn't go down in there anyway. They'd turn the hogs out and pick up all the heads and them hogs wouldcome out fat.

speaker: WALT
words: You remember how it used to be around the schock, it was all a bunchof heads. But with a combine it was a different story, they had to huntfor it.

speaker: NORLA
words: If you were binding a bundle, there was always a few heads that got awayfrom you on the ground, you know.

speaker: WALT
words: The hogs had a wonderful time they enjoyed it immensely but...(Laughs.)

speaker: NORLA
words: They were happy hogs. (Laughs.)

speaker: WALT
words: ...they didn't gain any weight.

speaker: NORLA
words: They were happy hogs.(Not transcribed.)NORLA: My grandmother used to make the best bean soup out of split beans.(Yeah.) Oh, she could really make good bean soup.ROB: In the early days, did people usually take their crops in and then buythem back after they were processed, or did they process them themselves?Like peas, would they...

speaker: NORLA
words: They kept enough at home. Like beans and stuff like that, we alwayskept beans enough to last all winter.

speaker: WALT
words: Oh, you mean before they was processed?

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah, we didn't process them we just kept a few beans at home. We didn'tbuy any.ROB: How would you clean them out?

speaker: NORLA
words: We just run 'em through the fanning mill or take *em out and spread thetarp down on the ground. We used to clean that way and wind them.

speaker: WALT
words: Everybody...

speaker: NORLA
words: You can do a good job when you wind them.

speaker: WALT
words: Everybody used to have a fanning mill.

speaker: NORLA
words: Oh, that fanning mill...

speaker: WALT
words: Everybody had a fanning mill and cleaned their own seed grain. (Yeah.)Anymore that don't take place.ROB: That's like winnowing?

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah, the regular screen and air. You do a real good job...

speaker: NORLA
words: You put hay in there and it goes around and the fan blows out and thecrap blows out the back end. We used to have dusters, treated our own wheat.Everybody got so lazy now that they don't do that. Easier to take it totown...

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. Buy all the seed wheat in town.

speaker: NORLA
words: No, we used to...I'll tell you, the farmer pret'near everything years agocome off'n the farm. (That's right.) We had our own milk and butter, cheese10and eggs, cured meat and raised our own vegetables and had our own beans,apples.

speaker: WALT
words: Prunes.

speaker: NORLA
words: Prunes, had dried...there used to be several prune dryers on the ridgehere.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah, there was three at one time wasn't there? Meyer, Johnson, andRussell.

speaker: NORLA
words: Old A. J. Russell. You remember when this place over here that Georgefarmed, the old Grandma Keene place, that was all apple trees at one time.(Yeah.) That packing house, you know there. (Yeah.) The old Taylor place.(Yeah.) It was a packing plant there in the back.

speaker: WALT
words: It was apples.

speaker: NORLA
words: The last apples I remember over here on this piece of Grandma Keene'swas in that draw that goes up where it goes into the old Carter place, goesup in that cove, you know on the hillside. Oh they was the best apples,they was Gravensteins. I used to go to school and there'd be three or fourtrees, go by there and the Brocke kids and I ud uld go up and get them apples.Boy there was the snappiest, they was just fall apples. (Yeah.) Boy theywere good.ROB: Did you both go to school at the schoolhouse that Frankie lives in now?

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. Well, not that building. Same place, but it was a different building,

speaker: NORLA
words: It was little further down the hill.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah, Just about the width of the thing down the hill.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's right. We used to play baseball on the upper side of the schoolhouseright whsre his house sets now. That's about where the baseball diamondwas. We used the back of the school as a backstop. (Laughs.)ROB: Did you break a few windows that way?

speaker: NORLA
words: No, there was no window on that back end, there was no windows on that end.11ROB: Were there quite a few sports and games and things played in school thosedays?

speaker: NORLA
words: Well, everyone of these ridges had a baseball team...

speaker: WALT
words: ...at one time.

speaker: NORLA
words: ...at one time there. Every part of this ridge had a baseball team.Bear Ridge, Cameron, Leland, Southwick, and every town of course.ROB: Would you go out and play each other?

speaker: NORLA
words: Oh yeah, we'd play on Sunday afternoon. They used to play over here overby the red barn didn't they. Right there in the flat.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. That was before my time.

speaker: NORLA
words: It was before my time but Wade Keene told me about it. (Yeah.) Theyplayed in that flat...

speaker: WALT
words: That's when Uncle Wallace lived there.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah, they played right across road there where George has got that patchhe put in oats. (Yeah.) Across the road and the creek there where the highway is now. (Yeah.) That was the baseball diamond. (Yeah.) Wade Keeneput me straight on that. They was playing baseball the afternoon I was born.(Yeah.) Wade said I was born and they was playing baseball over there.

speaker: WALT
words: I remember one time and that time I think they played up there at Keene's,out south of the building there.

speaker: NORLA
words: Out south of George's garage there.

speaker: WALT
words: I remember one time the Carlson boys came over here from Fix Ridge, andI suppose it would be the Fix Ridge team or...no, couldn't be Fix Ridge...

speaker: NORLA
words: Little Bear Ridge.

speaker: WALT
words: Little Bear. Yeah, that's where the Carlson's place was, wasn't it?

speaker: NORLA
words: Right across the street from...

speaker: WALT
words: Burnt Ridge.

speaker: NORLA
words: Burnt Ridge. It's between Bear Creek and Burnt Creek. Some people call12it Camp Creek now. That's right, Burnt Ridge.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. They used to come over here and...

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah, that old Carlson place was the house with the barn that set righton the edge of the canyon there. (Yeah.) Jim Trout lives there now. Youknow I haven't been there for years and years and years and one day last fall,Mabel and I had been to Moscow and she's always wanting to see Burnt Ridge,she's been as far down as the cemetery. (Yeah.) So we went down and lookedat the cemetery and I said let's go clear down to the end, clear down to Jim'splace, the old Carlson place. It must be three miles from there on down tothe end of the ridge. That's a nice laying ridge, the ground's beautiful.Pret'near level. We drove down there and turned around there by Jim's houseand went back.ROB: In these baseball games and stuff was there a lot of competition betweenthe ridges?

speaker: NORLA
words: Well, I think baseball is the only game they played.

speaker: WALT
words: Baseball is the only game people played.

speaker: NORLA
words: They all played baseball. They didn't have any practice. They didn'tpractice they just got their practice while they was playing.R0B: (Laughs.) Would one team walk over to another ridge and play there orwfeat?

speaker: WALT
words: Well, usually horseback or team and buggy.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's before cars happened.

speaker: WALT
words: Practically everybody rode horseback those days. (Yeah.) Instead ofhaving a motorcycle or a car you had a horse.

speaker: NORLA
words: Women and kids would go in the hack, and the young bucks, the baseballplayers, would have a horse. (That's right.) A saddle horse.ROB: What was it like in the one room school? I know like when you watch a T. V.show or something about those days the kids are always playing tricks like13slipping a frog in teachers desk or something like that. Did that kind ofstuff happen lot or was it a lot calmer than they showed it?

speaker: WALT
words: I'd say there was very little of it because the teachers seemed to alwayshave eyes in the back of her head.

speaker: NORLA
words: They wouldn't stand for that stuff.

speaker: WALT
words: They didn't stand for it. We had one old girl down there, you probablyremember, that had a buggy whip. She used it on the kids.

speaker: NORLA
words: I remember Sarah Sweeney, you remember she stayed with your folks. No,she boarded over here at the Jacobus' (Yeah.) I remember she had a cut-offbroomstick. She just cut the broom off and just had that stick. I'll tellyou that was a very effective message. (Laughs.) She'd just reach out andwham you with that thing. I saw her one time hit a kid right upside thehead with it.

speaker: WALT
words: You never went to school with Eliza Dallas did you?

speaker: NORLA
words: No, but I heard about her.

speaker: WALT
words: Boy, she knocked all the papers off my desk one time whipping old FrankMortimer. (Laughs.) Scared? I was just a little peter. Boy, I was sodamn scared I...(Stops, laughing.)

speaker: NORLA
words: I imagine you wasn't the only one scared you probably could have hearda pin drop.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. She sure give old Frank a going-over. (Laughs.) Hell, she hada twisted rawhide. Yeah, a twisted rawhide whip. Boy I'm a telling you whenthat come down it was really meant something.

speaker: NORLA
words: You betcha.ROB: Some of the boys would be really old wouldn't they? Sixteen, seventeen,around in there?

speaker: WALT
words: We got pictures in here. We got pictures someplace of kids going to school.They look like a buncha grown men.14

speaker: NORLA
words: They worn mustaches. Big long flowing mustaches. (Yeah.) We got oneup home there: George Davidson, and dad, and Jess Ball.

speaker: WALT
words: Some of the Brillhearts, I suppose.

speaker: NORLA
words: A Brillheart, and George Basher, and they all had big...grown men.ROB: Wouldn't she have a hard time keeping them in line?

speaker: NORLA
words: None of these guys were...they were all good gays. They never gave anytrouble.

speaker: WALT
words: Now the Basher boys they all, I think they all became physicians, didn'tthey?

speaker: NORLA
words: I think they were. I think they were.ROB: So they must have gotten some pretty good schooling those years...

speaker: NORLA
words: It was those two that I remember dad speaking of. There was Georgeand Lester Basher. And whether there was any more than that I don't know.

speaker: WALT
words: I think there was three of 'em.

speaker: NORLA
words: They lived on the Woody place. (Yeah.) That was the old Basher place.

speaker: WALT
words: One of the boys came back, that was when Pete was still alive, and stoppedand talked, but I can't remember which one it was.

speaker: NORLA
words: You see, if they was still alive the youngest of »em would be a hundredyears old.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. One of *em was a noted surgeon. Had a big name in California. Iforget now what town he was in. ,-

speaker: NORLA
words: I think he left here and went to California.

speaker: WALT
words: Oh, I'll tell you. There was some good people come out of the countryschools. You know what I mean? Educated people.

speaker: NORLA
words: Sotneways I think the kids in the country schools did a lot.

speaker: WALT
words: I think, I honestly think, that it was the worst mistake that thestate ever made when they consolidated the schools. I know it was the worstmistake financally.15

speaker: NORLA
words: They told us it was going to be less, but anybody if he had any senseat all knew that it couldn't be cheaper.

speaker: WALT
words: That's right. Taxes have done nothing but go up, school taxes.

speaker: NORLA
words: You're hiring a teacher, and you're hiring a bus driver, and you got abus.

speaker: WALT
words: Then you got maintenance on the bus.

speaker: NORLA
words: You got maintenance and everything and it just can't be cheaper. Andanother thing, the kids in the country...I know you went to it, we all wentto it. One teacher to eight grades. And I'll tell you she didn't have timeto spend much time on anybody. Just a few minutes to each class.ROB: Would the older kids help out with the younger kids sometimes?

speaker: NORLA
words: No, they were usually paddling their won canoe, getting their own lessons.But the kids down the line, that's where I think the country school had iton the other. You take a kid in the fifth grade. He'd set there and he'dlisten. He'd get his work done or maybe he was just listening, pickingdaisies, should have been a'studying but he'd listen. And he'd hear the kidsin the seventh and eighth grade reciting. (Yeah.) And he'd hear that beforehe ever got there. And when he got up there why he knew a lot of that stuff.That's where I think a lot of country schools by golly had it on the others.They could sit there and listen.

speaker: WALT
words: Why you probably remember when the state used to give eighth grade examinations...

speaker: NORLA
words: I took one of 'em.

speaker: WALT
words: ...and nine titoes out of ten it would be a country school that got thehighest grades. Some kid in the country school would come up with the highestgrade.ROB: How long would a teacher usually stay on in a country school? I know alot of school teachers came out, they were young women and they'd marry prettysoon.16

speaker: WALT
words: Well, it varied a lot. Now Sweeney, how long was she here? She was hereabout.•.

speaker: NORLA
words: Two years.

speaker: WALT
words: Two? I thought it was three.

speaker: NORLA
words: No, I had her in the fifth and sixth and Ann Deobald in the seventh andeighth.

speaker: WALT
words: I went to school with Sweeney I think two years. Do you know what TomGreen told me when he was down at Milton-Freewater? He had more or lessdiscipline trouble you know, in the school. And he said the country kidswere by far the best kids. The kids that came in out of the country. That'safter they'd consolidated. He said they were the best students and causedhim the least trouble.

speaker: NORLA
words: Well, I think a lot of that, Walt, the kids in the country had somethingto do.

speaker: WALT
words: Well, he said they'd go home at night and they all had chores to do, theyall had work to do.

speaker: NORLA
words: They all had responsibility.

speaker: WALT
words: The other kids would run around town and raise hell.

speaker: NORLA
words: Well they'd get a candy bar and two or three kids get together and donothing. (Yeah.) Why the country kids would get through school, they didn'thave school buses, you'd get on the cayuse and go home. And he had to milksome cows night and morning, and he had chores to do, he had something todo. (That's right.) Unemployment didn't bother him a bit when he had thingsto do. He didn't have time to get into trouble. 'Course on Halloween, wellsometimes he'd upset a privy or something. (Laughs.) But that's excuseableon Halloween, you know.ROB: I heard about other Halloween tricks like taking apart buggies and thingsand putting them inside the schoolhouse. Did they do that kind of stuff outhere?17

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah, they even put a cow inside the schoolhouse down here one time.(Laughs.) A steer. Did you ever know who did that? (Laughs.) I won'ttell 'til they're all dead. (Laughs.) I wasn't in the prank, I wasn't eventhere.

speaker: NORLA
words: But you heard about it.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah, I heard about it.ROB: I don't anybody would mind these days.

speaker: WALT
words: That was what you might call a shitty trick. (Laughs.)

speaker: NORLA
words: No, that wasn't very nice.ROB: Seems like most of tricks people played in those days really were a lotmore fun and a lot less mean, you know. They were more to make people laughthan to hurt people.

speaker: NORLA
words: Well I'll tell you I think the kids in them days, by golly, really Ithink they really really enjoyed themselves much...(End of Side A)

speaker: NORLA
words: Once in awhile we'd ski a little bit, but they mostly skated and...

speaker: WALT
words: Coasting.

speaker: NORLA
words: Coasting, you know, and then it would end up and we'd go to somebody'shouse from when the party broke up, you know. Go up to the house and haveoyster stew.ROB: Was this when you were little kids or...

speaker: NORLA
words: We were kids going to school.

speaker: WALT
words: Most all the grades. I don't think the real little kids ever turned out.

speaker: NORLA
words: First and second grade didn't, you know. 'Course we had kids here inthem days. I went over here when there was forty-five, and my dad went when'there was sixty. (Yeah.) You get a bunch like that and you can have a prettynice time of skating or coasting and all go up to the house and have an oysterfeed, oyster stew you know.18

speaker: WALT
words: Do you remember those old barrel staves that we used to go coastingon? Just take a barrel stave and put a block on it (laughing) and set ontop of the block about like a milking stool and try setting on...(Laughs.)

speaker: NORLA
words: You couldn't control it one bit you know.

speaker: WALT
words: (Laughing) I'll tell you, you got quite a thrill at times.

speaker: NORLA
words: And they used to have sleigh-ride parties, you remember that?

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. Toboggans. You remember when we used to ride over here on Deobald'scove?

speaker: NORLA
words: It's right over here where that big tree is, straight off that steep coveright over there.

speaker: WALT
words: It used to drift, the snow would drift up to the top of the hill six toeight feet high.

speaker: NORLA
words: You'd jump off that higher than your head.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. High as your head or higher. Come down over that thing, boy I'lltell you when you hit the ground. (Laughs.)

speaker: NORLA
words: You just nearly was flying.

speaker: WALT
words: Yep. That old board would crack like a rifle. That there, you had tobe careful or it would take your breath away, going that fast.ROB: When you started to get older, how did courtship and dating work in thecountry in the early days? What kind of things did you do then?

speaker: WALT
words: I think you just took your girl walking.

speaker: NORLA
words: You'd use the shank-pony. You used to go to church, you know, and theyoung fella would walk the gal home from church. (Yeah.) Sometimes itwould take quite a little while.

speaker: WALT
words: If you was lucky you'd have a team.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's right. They didn't hurry going home, you know, they had lotsof time. And them sleigh-ride parties. You'd take a big old bobsled andput some straw in there. If you're lucky enough to have a foot warmer to19try to keep your feet warm, you know. Blankets, the horse blankets, youknow. The horses with sleigh bells across the hames, you know. The skinnerhe was the driver, and the rest of 'em back in there of course bundled upto try to keep from freezing. 'Course the boys couldn't let the girls getcold.ROB: Of course not.

speaker: WALT
words: I remember one time we went to Troy. I forget how many there was of usbut I remember Nettie May was along. She was older than the rest of us.And coming home and I just had old Shorty and Tricks. There wasn't toomany of us but there was enough for a sleigh ride anyway. I can't rememberwhether we went to a dance or where we went to. Anyway, coming home I gotsleepy. I tied up the lines and crawled down under the covers and went to sleep,And Nettie May said we met two rigs in Bethel Gulch up there and the teamturned out for 'em, but I knew nothing about it. But she was older and shestayed awake.

speaker: NORLA
words: They'd turn out and let them go by. One time Harry and Ernie and I wentto Randall Flat to a dance. You've been out to Randall Flat haven't you?(Oh yeah.) One of the coldest places ever around to be, you know.

speaker: WALT
words: That's north of Troy.

speaker: NORLA
words: Northwest. (Yeah.) Out there northwest. Well we went up there Harryand I. Ernie had one pretty good horse and Harry had a pretty good one.So I come down, and Ernie and I went over. Harry had a sled. I harnessedup the team, Harry's horse and Ernie's horse, and we went to Randall Flatto a dance. Man it was below zero. I'll tell you we came awful near freezingto death. By golly, went up there and danced with those Swede gals you know.We used to have some fine times there. Once in awhile some rascal would bringin a little moonshine too you know. (Laughs.) Oh, it would crop in. AndAuntie...'course she was a sleeping in that bedroom right over there., .by20golly it got time for us to come home and we weren't home. Boy, she spentan awful night. It was broad daylight and she was getting worried and shekept a'looking and she just knew something would happen to them guys. Andshe said it was broad daylight and she said I looked out and here come Ernieand I leading the old mare (laughs) walking home with that there horse andsled on it. Harry in there, we come in there in broad daylight. Cuttingacross the field. Oh shucks, that's what you call young and irresponsibile.ROB: When a man kinda decided on a woman he thought he might like to marrywould the courtship take quite awhile?

speaker: NORLA
words: (Laughs.) That depends.

speaker: WALT
words: I'll tell you, in our time we didn't get married very young. I think Iwas 28. How old were you Bub?

speaker: NORLA
words: Going on 32. Do you remember the Harvest Balls they used to have downhere at the Temple? They used to have a Harvest Ball every Fall. (Oh yeah.)

speaker: NORLA
words: Boy, that was some dance. (Yeah.) They'd decorate the hall up withpumpkin and corn stalk or shocks of corn, you know. Mann Brothers, youremember them? That was a good orchestra. (Yeah.) After they outgrowedLewiston here, they played big time. They played Seattle and San Franciscoand places like that in the big hotels. They were tops. Yeah, Mann Brothersplayed there and then there was another after Mann Brothers went into thebig circuit. Why then there was Kaufman's. That was a good orchestra, youknow. Kaufman's used to come up here. We used to have the best times downthere, you know, in the fall. The harvest was all done and they'd have a bigharvest ball. Man, I don't know why it didn't fall down. (Laughs.) Ilook at it now, the way the crowds used to get in there. They had somewonderful times there.

speaker: WALT
words: It's a wonder that that old temple didn't cave in. I saw that floorcovered with people. (That's right.) They condemned it. Why it didn't break21down I don't know. (I don't know.) Still condemned I guess isn't it?

speaker: NORLA
words: Well they got some steel posts in down below there. It's pretty goodnow.ROB: I've been hearing something about after people got married their neighborswould throw a shivaree for them. Did that happen much up here?

speaker: NORLA
words: Oh, it never failed. We always had a shivaree, you know. You justfigure on buying treats.

speaker: WALT
words: Shotguns and the works.ROB: What would a shivaree be? What would people do in a shivaree?

speaker: WALT
words: Oh just make all the cockeyed noise you can make. That's was all the...what it really should have been is music, you know. What would you call it?

speaker: NORLA
words: Just kinda serenade*- 'em.

speaker: WALT
words: Serenade. It wasn't a serenade. It was just a bunch of noise.ROB: Was that the night they got married or anytime...

speaker: WALT
words: Well, first night you caught 'em home.

speaker: NORLA
words: When they got home from their homeymoon, you know. Why the couplealways knew they was coming. And they'd have candy bars, you know, and applesand different things for treats, you know, and coffee. And they'd move in.The crowd, they always liked to wait till they got in bed if they could.Wait late, you know. Sneak in, you know, and that noise would turn looseoutside. There was always some rascal or two would have his shotgun. Getright beside the house and shoot right up in the air like that. Tin cansand stuff. Remember that old horsefiddle you used to have? That old rigyou they turned with a crank they called a horsefiddle.

speaker: WALT
words: Do you know...?

speaker: NORLA
words: Bump, Bump, Bump, Bump. It was a cog. It run on a cog like that. Oh,it was the noisest thing.

speaker: WALT
words: I think I still got it.21

speaker: NORLA
words: You have!

speaker: WALT
words: I think it's up there in this old shed.

speaker: NORLA
words: It is!

speaker: WALT
words: I think so.

speaker: NORLA
words: By god you oughta hang on to that. That thing will be something. Thatmade the awfullest noise. And they'd turn that thing and crank and there wasa thing that run on a cog and jumped from on cog to another. Oh brother,talk about noise. They'd get 'em up out of bed, you know, and they'd invite'em in, you know. That was the main idea, to get 'em up. Then they'd setaround, you know, and visit and eat candy bars and apples. Just have anice evening.

speaker: NORLA
words: WALTr Well I didn't remember...my memory ain't so good any more...but KyleAnderson told me the other day, you know when we were shivareed. I don'tknow whether you came or not.

speaker: NORLA
words: Well, I imagine.

speaker: WALT
words: The Woody boys was there and Kyle and I can't remember who all. Butanyway he knew we weren't prepared for 'em. So he came and told me.(Laughs.)

speaker: NORLA
words: You better get something, huh?

speaker: WALT
words: So I went to town and got some cigars and candy bars and got prepared forthe shivaree. It took place all right.

speaker: NORLA
words: Got the treats.

speaker: WALT
words: We had ice cream and cake.

speaker: NORLA
words: I don't know. In them days the people was happy. (Oh yeah.) In someways I think they was happier than they are now.

speaker: WALT
words: Oh, happier.

speaker: NORLA
words: People got so much now that they should be happy but they just got somuch that they...I know one thing, we used to visit a lot more.22

speaker: WALT
words: Oh, have pinochle parties.

speaker: NORLA
words: Oh, they'd have parties you know. People would go to church and theirneighbor would invite 'em home for dinner, and we'd invite somebody hometo our place. People visited. And they had parties you know, and literary.You remember when we used to have literary when we was going to school.

speaker: WALT
words: Literary!

speaker: NORLA
words: Programs and by golly, now you never see your neighbor. Well, you yaveat him across the draw or across the fence. He's on one tractor and you'reon the other, and wave at him going or coming from town. (Yeah.) Butactually as far as going over to a neighbor and visit and spend the day andeat dinner with him it's pret'near unheard of. (Yeah.) They get in the carand drive fifty or a hundred miles somewhere to visit somebody.

speaker: WALT
words: That went out when the cars came in.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's right. But when they had horses and the old buggy or the hack,boy you didn't get too far away from home.ROB: Did they used to have those kitchen sweats out here?

speaker: NORLA
words: Oh sure. We'd get together and dance in the kitchen. Boys would bedressed in overalls, you know. And just one guy would play the squeezeboxand the other play the fiddle. That's why it's called kitchen sweat.ROB: Because everybody gets in there and sweats.

speaker: NORLA
words: Well, they were in that place and they is so thick in there, get hot,and they'd get up a sweat, you know. That's pretty good exercise. (Laughs.)But they had a lot of fun. I don't know maybe they enjoyed it better thanwe do now. It didn't take so much thero days to make people happy. People'sgot so now it takes an awful lot to make them happy. You could have a goodtime at a kitchen sweat in a pair of overalls. In fact, that's about allthey ever wore. The men just wore new washed pair of overalls. (Yeah.)Clean shirts, you know. No admission I don't think was ever charged. Maybe23they passed the hat to pay the fiddler and the guy playing the squeezebox.Remember, they used to have a dance over at Sam Bigham's over at the...(Oh yeah.) ...Boy I can see that old fiddler...(Old granary) I can see thatold fiddler setting up there yet. I was just a kid. Dad and mother used togo. Just as plain as can be I can see. It was a pretty good size hall.(Yeah.) That was a lot bigger than the kitchen. They could really callquite a dance there.

speaker: WALT
words: I wonder if the old building still stands. I think it does.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah. It's still standing. Don't look in too bad of shape either.

speaker: WALT
words: And barn dances...we used to have barn dance once in awhile. Rememberthey had quite a series of barn dances down there where Sneeder Brammerlives. Do you remember that?

speaker: NORLA
words: They turned that into quite a dance business.

speaker: WALT
words: Were you there the night the floor broke through? (No.) One night thefloor broke through. I don't know if they had any more after that or not.I'll tell you who was living there it was...Compton.

speaker: NORLA
words: Stuart.

speaker: WALT
words: Stuart Compton.

speaker: NORLA
words: He was always out to making a dollar, you know. (Yeah.) He turned itinto a dance pavilion.(Not transcribed.)ROB: Would there be big parties and things too, after they finished thrashingat one farm? Would there be a party there to celebrate that?

speaker: NORLA
words: No, they just moved to another farm.ROB: How many thrashing rigs were there on this end of the ridge?

speaker: WALT
words: What was that?24ROB: How many thrashing rigs...

speaker: NORLA
words: How many thrashing machines were there?

speaker: WALT
words: One year, maybe more than one year, there was only one. Old ChrisMeyer, you remember. (Yeah.) The fall the grain got wet. He thrashed thewhole ridge that year. But the next year, that was when the company machinewas...

speaker: NORLA
words: Well then before the company machine you remember George Clem used tothrash up here. (Yeah. George Clem.) He thrashed for us a number ofyears. Warney May used to thrash. (Yeah.) And Jess Ball.

speaker: WALT
words: But Wareny used to go over on the Genesee country too.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah. He used pull up and go over there, too.

speaker: WALT
words: They'd get bigger run over there.

speaker: NORLA
words: He thrashed a time or two for dad. (Yeah.) But George Clem thrashed forus several years. And then way back, as the Indian says, a long time ago,Martin Thomas run the machine.

speaker: WALT
words: Martin used to thrash the lower end of the ridge.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah, he used to thrash for us. I remember more than once. (Yeah.)He thrashed for us up there. By golly, he never whistled. He always saidthat he had a thrashing machine not a whistling machine.ROB: The whistle was to stop work?

speaker: NORLA
words: Well, any kind of signal or anything, you'd always whistle. You'd stop,blow the whistle for noon. They toot it a little different when they stopped,you know. Some guys moving along the road and see somebody, they'd pull thewhistle and toot at him. Martin said he had a thrashing machine. Now theywas setting there thrashing at home. The engine was setting just about whereBob's corral is. Except it didn't go as far as that stack bottom there, anddad was doing the milking, him and I. I was just a little shaver, I think Imilked one cow or two. We were doing the milking and the guys went in the25house to eat breakfast. When we got through milking, dad says, "We're gonnahear that whistle." Dad got up and pulled the whistle back and tied it.

speaker: WALT
words: Oh no! (Everybody laughing.)

speaker: NORLA
words: Tied it down. By golly, mother said...Martin see was mother's brother...(Yeah I know he was.) ...if it had been aiybody else but dad he'd have killedninu (Laughs.) Hard to clean up on his brother-in-law. And mother saidMartin just set, that big old mustache of his, you know, pulling food in.He just sneezed and snorted but he never got up from his breakfast. But shesaid the minute he got through, God help it he went out and unwired thatwhistle. (Laughs.)

speaker: WALT
words: He lowered the steam didn't he?

speaker: NORLA
words: He sure did. Yeah. He just tied her down. Pulled her back and wrappedthe wire around the lever, let her go. Yeah, he'd have killed if it had beenanybody but his brother-in-law. (Laughs.) Yeah, most of the time there wastwo machines on the ridge.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. I don't know maybe there was only one year that this Chris Meyerthrashed...

speaker: NORLA
words: One time there were three. There was Chris Meyer, and the Company machine,and then that little company rig that Perry...

speaker: WALT
words: Oh yeah. I remember that.

speaker: NORLA
words: ...and Hal and them were in on. At one time there were three thrashingmachines.

speaker: WALT
words: That was right at the end of thrashing.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's right.

speaker: WALT
words: The next thing was combines.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah. That was just before combine come in.ROB: Would it work that somebody would just go out and buy a thrashing machineand then rent it out to help pay it off or what?26

speaker: NORLA
words: Well, they just bought it and they went out and cut, and what they made...

speaker: WALT
words: I don't think anybody ever went busted here with a thrashing machine ifthat's what you mean.

speaker: NORLA
words: No, people here was pretty good about paying the thrash bill. (Yeah.)Some thrashermen would go broke if they didn't pay their bills.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. They'd go broke in a hurry if they couldn't collect their...

speaker: NORLA
words: People here, you know, paid their thrash bills. Before the combine therewas three thrashing machines. Two company rigs.ROB: Those company rigs worked that you bought in shares on the machines?

speaker: NORLA
words: There was six of 'em in our rig. It started out Byard Davidson andWade Keene, Walt Bigham, George and Frank Roberts, and dad. And when Wadequit he sold to Ira. (Oh, to Ira!) Ira Haven. And Bigham sold to EmilOlson. You remember when Bigham had to go to the army? He sold to Emil,and Byard, I don't know whether he sold or what the arrangement was, butanyway Cliff come up. (Yeah.) He wound up in the rig. But that was theorginal six was the one's I named first. Dad and Frank and George, theywere in it all the time. From the start to the finish. Bought that oldmachine the fall of '16. I'll never forget that. Granddad and I went toElk River on a fishing trip with the old Maxwell. There was an old AltmanTaylorThrasher, a 27-inch Altman-Taylor, pretty good old machine that was.And that old International tractor that burnt distillate, you remember?(Yeah.) The pistons was down this way. (Yeah.) George run the separator andLeslie run main. They got that machine while we was at Elk River fishingwith the old Maxwell for a couple of days. When we come home, why the oldman Guy, remember had that place down there...(Yeah.)...on the grade...(Yeah.)...and had it in wheat, good piece of wheat about 50 acres of it.And he prevailed on the boys to thrash his wheat while they was coming upthe grade, bringing it home. And they pulled in there and thrashed there27first. First thrashing they done was that piece on the Guy place. Andthey come on up on the hill and stayed there for the rest of the time.

speaker: WALT
words: That was the first bulking too that was ever done.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's right. That's what introduced bulk wheat to this country.(Yeah.) Each company man furnished a bundle wagon and a tank to haul thewheat.

speaker: WALT
words: That was a big saving.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah, each company owner had a bundle wagon and hauled the wheat. Andthey traded, you know. There was six of »em and they each had a tank andfour or five horses on a wagon. They just run under the spout and hauled itbulk, you know. And at the end of the year they got the weights, set downand had a grand and glorious settling-up some night down at George Davidson's.They figured out what their expenses was and how much it was going to cost.And each man was assessed that much according to the amount of wheat he had.A man that had 2000 bushels of wheat would pay more than a man that had 1200bushels. They figured out on that basis, just as fair as they could be.(Why sure it was.) What their expenses was against what amount he had thrashed.And then the difference in the bundle wagon, why that was trade-work, backand forth.

speaker: WALT
words: A fella who had a small crop would come out long on the hauling...(That'sright.)...he'd get paid for hauling.

speaker: NORLA
words: He hauled more for the other guy than they did for him, you see? Sayone guy had 1500 bushels of wheat and the neighbor over here had 3000. Well,when they traded why he'd get a little back. (Yeah.) On the bundle wagonhauling they paid so much for wagon and team to haul bundles. Well, theysay it was four or five dollars a day. I remember when it got five dollarsthat was a awful price.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. Four dollars was...28

speaker: NORLA
words: Let's say I worked for Walt three days, I hauled bundle for him forthree days. Well he hauled for me two and a half days. I owe him a half aday, see? That's the way it figured out. It wasn't hard to figure out.

speaker: WALT
words: I think four dollars was the going wages for a man, a team, and a bundlewagon.

speaker: NORLA
words: I remember when it got up to five dollars. Lester Crocker was pitchingthat year. We thought that was awful, five dollars for a pitcher. Hepitched bundles one year, five dollars a day.

speaker: WALT
words: That was after things started to pick up. (That's right.) That waswar days. (That's right.)ROB: In the days before this company machine came in and people traded workaround thrashing time, did people ever take advantage of that or was everybodypretty straight with their neighbors. Would some people be difficult tosettle up with?

speaker: WALT
words: I don't ever remember having trouble with anybody.

speaker: NORLA
words: These people here were all honest. We didn't have any...

speaker: WALT
words: Up until the time that old Perry Huddleston moved over here on the cornernobody ever locked anything. There was: nothing ever locked up.

speaker: NORLA
words: You didn't ever have to worry about anybody bothering anything.

speaker: WALT
words: You didn't worry because nobody took anything. But then it started.They started coming in. Stealing started taking place and nowadays it ain'ttoo good.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah, about that time...you know every once in awhile you'd loose a hamor a side or two of bacon. Things started moving around. (Yep.) But thepeople lived here, the old original settlers, you didn't have to worry athing about. They was just as honest as they could be. You didn't have toworry about a thing.ROB: I've heard stories about how up in the Princeton country say like a ham29would disappear or something and if the people would know who it was they'dgo and take care of it themselves. How did it work around here?

speaker: WALT
words: We never taken care of old Perry but he should have been taken care of.(Laughs.)

speaker: NORLA
words: We knew where it was a'going. Remember when they found those hams overthere on the wood pile over there on the Jennings place? (No. I don't know.)Did you hear about that? (No.) I supposed you knew about it. It was aboutthe time that they were losing meat. I forget who it was that went overthere, cutting wood, and they found a ham or two cached away in a pile wherethey pile limb wood up, where they'd been making wood you know. (Yeah.)They found it next spring. It was rotten. Pretty good idea who put it there.It got pretty tight you see, pretty hot. They just left it there.ROB: How did people try to enforce the law...

speaker: WALT
words: Oh hell, I never remember a time when we didn't have a sheriff.ROB: What would people do then in case something went wrong?

speaker: NORLA
words: I don't know.ROB: In case there was some kind of fight you know.

speaker: NORLA
words: We never had any trouble like that.

speaker: WALT
words: We never had any trouble here on the ridge.

speaker: NORLA
words: You remember the time old Perry got into Wade Keene's cellar under thehouse where George lived?

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah, but you know he had that trap set for him.

speaker: NORLA
words: By golly, you know when Wade and them were going to Moscow...

speaker: WALT
words: Decoration Day wasn't it?

speaker: NORLA
words: Decoration Day and he rigged up a shotgun in there. By golly, who was itthat was plowing out there with a walking plow on that place that Dick farms.

speaker: WALT
words: I don't think there was anybody. I was planting beans.

speaker: NORLA
words: Somebody, maybe it was you, and they heard the gun go off.30

speaker: WALT
words: I heard the gun go off but I thought...the wind was blowing like the deviland I thought it was a board blew off you know and come down flat. I nevereven looked, I just kept on planting beans.

speaker: NORLA
words: Well anyway, when Wade and them come home that afternoon, the first thingby god Wade thought of was go down to see how his trap had worked. Heopened the door and there stood Perry. (Laughs.) He-had him. He couldn'tget out see.ROB: If the gun had already gone off why couldn't he get out?

speaker: NORLA
words: It set the trap. The gun went off, by golly, and closed the door andhe couldn't get it open.ROB: Without setting the gun off again.

speaker: NORLA
words: Well, unless you opened it from the outside. Wade said, "I opened thedoor and there be set, blinking his eyes in the dark, blinking his eyeslooking at me. 'What are you doing here in my cellar?'"ROB: What did he say?

speaker: NORLA
words: I don't know. I imagine he said quite a bit.

speaker: WALT
words: Wade came down and asked me if I heard that gun go off, and I scratchedmy head awhile and I told him no. I said I thought I heard a board blowoff of something...

speaker: NORLA
words: You heard something slap.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. But you know I knew they were gone. (Yeah.) If Wade had come andtold me. I would have been a looking for it. I'd been a'looking.

speaker: NORLA
words: How did Perry get up there? Did he wait until you got on the otherside of the hill?

speaker: WALT
words: He must of waited 'til I got over the hill.

speaker: NORLA
words: You was on the road side.

speaker: WALT
words: I was going back and forth like this.

speaker: NORLA
words: You was on the other side of the field where you couldn't see him and he31sneaked up there.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. And he just slip on the road there. He knew old Wade was gone.

speaker: NORLA
words: Why sure. He had to go by his place, you see, to go to Moscow.ROB: How would people handle something like that? I mean would he just let himgo, or turn him over to the sheriff in town?

speaker: NORLA
words: No he just...

speaker: WALT
words: I think Wade just kicked his ass. (Laughs.)

speaker: NORLA
words: I think he just opened up a boot shop (laughs) and let it go at that.No, they never got anything.

speaker: WALT
words: They didn't have anything.

speaker: NORLA
words: Poor as Job's turkey.

speaker: WALT
words: A whole bunch of kids.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah, several children.

speaker: WALT
words: At least he let old Perry know that...

speaker: NORLA
words: We never had trouble like that.

speaker: WALT
words: You couldn't do stuff like that.

speaker: NORLA
words: People never had trouble anything like that very much.(Not transcribed.)ROB: Was it a pretty religious community in those tiroes?

speaker: WALT
words: Oh it was about fifty-fifty.

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah, there usad to be two churches here, Catholic and one on the hill.

speaker: WALT
words: There were two churches here at one time.ROB: A Catholic Church and a Luthern Church?

speaker: NORLA
words: No it was the Nazerenes and the Methodists.

speaker: WALT
words: The church was built for a community church, but the Methodists took itover and they wouldn't let the other people use their church, so they fixedup an old packing house that it used to stand over here.

speaker: NORLA
words: You know that was pretty small at that.

speaker: WALT
words: Oh small, Norla that was terrible. They should have bent over backwardsbut they didn't do it.

speaker: NORLA
words: The people built it for a community church.

speaker: WALT
words: That was absolutely built for a community church. (Yep.)(End of Side B)(Not transcribed.)

speaker: WALT
words: Closed, they were all closed. I know I went up to Clarkia one time thefourth of July. I had an uncle living up there and booze was around on thetable. I guess you would call it a cafe at that time, and they was playingcards and there was booze on the table. And everything was closed up downhere.ROB: Did the town itself vote on whether it wanted to be open or closed ?

speaker: WALT
words: Oh it was state wasn't it?

speaker: NORLA
words: Yeah, it was local option they called it.

speaker: WALT
words: Well, anyway you see Bovill's in Latah Country, and Latah County kept asheriff there in Bovill pratically all the time although there was a lotof whiskey floating around when the sheriff was there.ROB: That was Pat Malone wasn't it?

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah.

speaker: NORLA
words: He was as big as a horse. (Yeah. That's right.) Remember Pat alwayswore that big overcoat and he always had a pint in it. (Yeah.) He couldtake care of most of the whiskey there was up there. He used to come downto the dances when they had the dance halls, Pat would.

speaker: NORLA
words: Here in Prohibition, you know, why you could always get a bottle of3233whiskey. They'd call local option. They'd get it down at the druggist here.Dad used to go in there. I imagine a pint of whiskey would last Dad foryears. (Yeah.) He'd just set it on the medicine shelf for when he wouldwant a little cough syrup, you know,a little pint of whiskey. You could getJimmy Durbin Whiskey. He's the one that had on the bottle "Don't Buy BoozeIf Your Kids Need Shoes." You remember that Walt? (Yeah.) And dad wouldgo down here to Albert Moskoff the druggist, and he'd get that from Albert.ROB: Was that made around here or was that shipped in from some place?

speaker: WALT
words: Oh no, it was bonded whiskey.

speaker: NORLA
words: It was bonded whiskey, it wasn't moonshine.

speaker: WALT
words: There was lots of moonshine flowed into Kendrick and just where it camefrom I don't know.

speaker: NORLA
words: It got here anyway.

speaker: WALT
words: It came in. One time you could buy it without any trouble. I kind ofthink...who in the devil was it. Old Archie May went into the drug storeand got a bottle of formaldehyde and took it across the street and and putit in his buggy, (laughs) in the back of his buggy. Somebody saw himand he supposed it was whiskey. And so he went over and slipped the bottleout and took a big snort. I cari't think of who in the devil it was.

speaker: NORLA
words: It's a wonder it didn't kill him.

speaker: WALT
words: Who in the hell was that who took the snort of formaldehyde? I'll askFrankie someday.

speaker: NORLA
words: He used it treating wheat you know. If you got a little too much inthere it would kill the wheat. I wouldn't germinate.

speaker: WALT
words: I don't imagine the guy could take more than one swallow (laughs).I don't know if he could even swallow it or not. You got it in your mouthit would...

speaker: NORLA
words: It would burn like fire.34ROB: Up further north up on the ridge and down in Troy there was some hardfeelings between the Swedish people and the Norwegians. Was that a problemdown in this area too?

speaker: WALT
words: Oh no, no problem at all.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's why they called it American Ridge down here. Few problems atall here. Everybody got along fine.ROB: It's pretty remarkable because it seems like most of the places in thecounty had some problems.

speaker: NORLA
words: No trouble at all. About the best neighorhood you could ever ask to bein.

speaker: WALT
words: Before our time Bub, there was some trouble. Old Charley Jessop he wasan ornery old devil.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's right. He was very religious too.

speaker: WALT
words: Oh yes. I'm trying to think of one that...there was a place and it wouldbe Jessop's south forty.

speaker: NORLA
words: That's the one next to the Dougharty place.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. Some guy lived down there, who was it? Was his name Hammer?

speaker: NORLA
words: I don't know if that's the name but there was a man by the name ofHammer that lived here. But I don't know where he lived. I remember Dadspeaking of Hammer. But I don't know where he lived at.

speaker: WALT
words: Anyhow, I think that was the place, and he and Charley were talking. Hehad to go to Moscow to prove up up on his place, you know, to sign the finalpapees or something. I don't know just what the deal was, but Charley convincedhim there was no need for him to go, because he had to go and he'd take careof it. And he went out and he took care of it. But he wound up with theforty.ROB: How could he do something like that?

speaker: WALT
words: I don't know. I just don't know. And whoever told me was in the know35and I can't remember now who in the devil it was that told me. He said itkilled the old boy, he brooded over it and...

speaker: NORLA
words: Sure didn't kill Charley.

speaker: WALT
words: No, it didn't hurt Charley a bit. And there's another one old Charleypulled. He pulled some good ones.ROB: What were the early homesteader like? Why did they come out here?

speaker: NORLA
words: The promised land.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah, that's right. It was the promised land.

speaker: NORLA
words: Most of 'em that come here was bachelors. Homesteaded and proved upand if they had a family they brought the family out in a few years. Somemight have met some girl and got married. Tbey were pretty much...all ofthem that I remember come here in the first...there was George Dougharty,and John Roberts, Sam Bigham, Frank May, Martin Thomas, and all them. Theywere all bachelors.

speaker: WALT
words: Was old Frank a bachelor?

speaker: NORLA
words: When he first come here I think he was. They was all bachelors. Yousee John Roberts, and Sam Bigham, George Dougharty come here together. Iremember we was thrashing wheat one time down at the ranch there, and Georgecome out and climbed up on my truck. And I said to him I said,"Mr. Dougharty" I said, "just when did you come here?"He said, "We come in the fall of '77." I remember it was 101 yearsafter the Declaration of Independence.I said, "Who come?"He said, "Sam Bigham, and John right up here, and myself." And he said,"We settled right here joining each other."ROB: Did they know each other when they came?

speaker: NORLA
words: Oh yeah, they come together. They come here together, the three bachelors.They settled right here together and he said, "The reason I took this place36here, it was awful steep ground" he said, "I took it on account of water."Sam he had a spring over there where he settled.

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah. He had water.

speaker: NORLA
words: He had water. I don't know about John. John wasn't very far fromGeorge, maybe he had a spring up there. I don't know. (No, I don't...)George told me he says, "That's why I settled here was on account of thewater. There was water every where." They come here together. And thenMartin, I don't know Martin come here a year or two after that and FrankMay come the next fall. He said, "We was here one year before Frank May."That wasn't exactly the way Mays said it when they had that piece. (No.)It wasn't exactly like what George Dougharty said, but I think George had itright.

speaker: WALT
words: George had it right. He was as honest as the day is long.

speaker: NORLA
words: There wasn't a honester man living than George Dougharty. He said,"We come the year before Frank May."

speaker: WALT
words: Well, Martin had some land over at Genesee, didn't he?

speaker: NORLA
words: That's right.

speaker: WALT
words: Didn't he have a place over there?

speaker: NORLA
words: He homesteaded over there. It was between Genesee and Uniontown.

speaker: WALT
words: How did he do that? Did he sell that and buy this over here?

speaker: NORLA
words: He sold that and come over here (Yeah?) That's how he come over here.It was two or three years after George and Sam and John come. He sold hishomestead and he come over and bought that land down there and he took thaton account of water. Water every where you look there, you know.

speaker: WALT
words: He got oodles of water. And Dougharty got plenty of water. And Bigham gotplenty of water, but Bigham's water run out. (Yeah.) Had to drill a well.(Yeah.) Water table's lowering.

speaker: NORLA
words: Bigham used to have water in the basement of the house, you remember?37

speaker: WALT
words: Oh yeah. Had a pump right in the kitchen.

speaker: NORLA
words: Had a pump right in the kitchen.

speaker: WALT
words: Pump in the kitchen pumped it right up...

speaker: NORLA
words: Pump 'er right out of the basement of the house.

speaker: WALT
words: Pumped 'er out of the basement.

speaker: NORLA
words: The water level went down.

speaker: WALT
words: An old pitcher pump. I remember that.ROB: Did you ever talk to Dougharty or any or those folks about what the land waslike and what the work was like the first few years they were here?

speaker: NORLA
words: It was all bunchgrass prairies down where they were. Bunchgrass hereon the south slope, but these draws all had timber in 'em.ROB: They didn't have to clear anything then...

speaker: WALT
words: Bunchgrass and road brush. (That's right.) And I'll tell you somethingelse that was quite prevalent was sunflowers.

speaker: NORLA
words: Oh yeah. Sunflowers all over, growing in the bunchgrass.

speaker: WALT
words: Your dad told me...

speaker: NORLA
words: They had stalk has big as my wrist.

speaker: WALT
words: Your dad told roe he said, "It took a good team to pull a plow through asunflower stalk." Yeah, they were great big, heavy, tough...

speaker: NORLA
words: Was you over there at the cemetery here a number of years ago when weworked that cemetery over? And remember Harley had one of these little JohnDeere two cylinder crawler rigs, you remember that? (Yeah.) I'll neverforget that. We moved the-^stones all off, of course had a plot of thecemetery, and there were trees in that and bushes in that 20 feet high.Pulled »em and hauled 'em down there south on that rock side hill. I rememberthere was a big old sunflower there and that sunflower stuck that littletractor. It would set there and spin the tracks. It had a blade on it.Them things were tough. He had a plow, it would pull a two-bottom plow you38know. By golly, he had a blade and he was going to bulldoze it out withthat blade and he just set and spin his tracks. Them old sunflowers aretough. They were all over the south slopes in the bunchgrass, bunchgrasssod prairie.ROB: Did they have to break and turn the sod when they first started?

speaker: WALT
words: I don't think bunchgrass was very soddy. (No, no.) It's just what itsays it is, bunchgrass you know, it don't build sod up...

speaker: NORLA
words: Just a bunch here and a bunch there, you know.

speaker: WALT
words: Grows in bunches.

speaker: NORLA
words: About so far apart (indicating one to two feet).

speaker: WALT
words: Yeah, they used three horese on a breaking plow.

speaker: NORLA
words: Three horses and a footburner.

speaker: WALT
words: I don't know how big the plow was, probably 14-inch.

speaker: NORLA
words: I believe it was 14-inch. (Yeah.) I know Dad said he broke all thisplace up except for about ten acres with three Indian cayuses. He used togo down to get Indian cayuses from Indians, get them for ten dollars a piece.Broke it up with a footburner and three cayuses.

speaker: WALT
words: Your dad broke up most of this place, didn't he? Yeah, I think he did.I think he broke this up too. How did...Geer had this before Frank Robertsgot it.

speaker: NORLA
words: Well .granddad bought it from Tom Geer.

speaker: WALT
words: Oh, he bought it from Tom Geer.

speaker: NORLA
words: Geer, you see, proved up on it. (Oh well...) Then he left here and granddad bought him out.

speaker: WALT
words: Geer built the barn. (Yes.) Because Frank told me that.

speaker: NORLA
words: Geer built the barn,

speaker: WALT
words: Well this place must have been broke up then.39

speaker: NORLA
words: It must have been. I know granddad broke ours up all but about ten acres.

speaker: NORLA
words: Transcribed and typed by Sherrie Fields.
